questions.prompt: What core problem does Exclaimer solve for an MSP customer?
choices: It scans inbound email for phishing and malware
It centralises signature templates and applies them across mailboxes without per-user setup
It replaces Microsoft 365 as the customer's mail provider
It encrypts outbound email at the gateway
questions.explanation: Exclaimer pulls signatures off individual mailboxes and into a central template managed by the MSP, so brand updates and disclaimers don't drift across users or devices.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid Exclaimer deployment paths for Microsoft 365? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Server-side via an Exchange Online connector and transport rule
Client-side via the Exclaimer Outlook Add-in
Client-side via the Exclaimer Signature Update Agent on Windows
Direct mailbox modification through Graph API
Both server-side and client-side configured together for the same subscription
questions.explanation: Exclaimer supports server-side, client-side via the Outlook Add-in (recommended), client-side via the Signature Update Agent, and the combined mode where both run together. It does not modify mailboxes directly via Graph.

questions.prompt: When both client-side and server-side are configured for the same subscription, the client-side rendering takes priority on supported mail clients; server-side fills the gap on devices the Add-in doesn't run on.
questions.explanation: Exclaimer's documentation calls out exactly this priority. Client-side wins where the Outlook Add-in is present; server-side covers mobile, web in some cases, and any client without the Add-in.

questions.prompt: Where does the Diagnostic Logs feature live in the Exclaimer portal?
choices: Sidebar, under Analytics
Header bar, under the cogwheel Settings menu
Header bar, under the question-mark Help menu
Header bar, under the initials Account menu
questions.explanation: Diagnostic Logs and Message Capture both live under the question-mark Help menu, alongside the Signatures Tester shortcut and the Raise a Support Ticket form.

questions.prompt: MSP staff confirm the active subscription in the header before making any change for a customer.
questions.explanation: The most expensive MSP mistake is editing the wrong customer's signature because the subscription switcher landed somewhere else. The header is the canonical check before any edit.

questions.prompt: Which of these does the Connect to Microsoft 365 wizard create in Exchange Online? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A Send connector for outbound to Exclaimer
A Receive connector for the signed message return
A transport rule named 'Identify messages to send to Exclaimer Cloud'
An accepted domain in the form tenantid.smtp.excl.cloud
A new Exchange mailbox for the Exclaimer service
questions.explanation: The wizard creates two connectors, the transport rule, and the accepted domain. It does not create a mailbox; Exclaimer never holds messages in an Exchange mailbox.

questions.prompt: Exclaimer caches the Microsoft 365 Global Administrator credentials so the wizard can re-run automatically when needed.
questions.explanation: Exclaimer explicitly does not cache the GA credentials. Any wizard rerun prompts the GA again. This is in the Security and Compliance FAQ.

questions.prompt: A user's signature shows their job title as blank. What's the right fix?
choices: Hardcode the title into the template's Text element
Update the directory record (or have the user edit it via the User Details Editor)
Delete the {JobTitle} field from the template
Re-run the Connect to Microsoft 365 wizard
questions.explanation: Field elements pull from the synced directory at send time. The fix is to populate the directory data; hardcoding breaks for the next user with the same template.

questions.prompt: Which Toolbox elements pull per-user data from the directory at send time? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Personal {Fields}
Contact {Fields}
Address {Fields}
All {Fields}
Disclaimer element
questions.explanation: The four Fields elements all pull from the directory. The Disclaimer element uses static text from the active Brand Kit, not per-user data.

questions.prompt: The Signatures Tester does which of the following?
choices: Sends a real email to the recipient and inspects what arrives
Simulates how a chosen From and To pair land against every enabled signature, without sending mail
Runs an Exchange Online message trace for the chosen sender
Updates user data in the synced directory
questions.explanation: The Tester is a simulation. It does not send mail. It evaluates each enabled signature's rules against the chosen sender and recipient and reports which would have applied.

questions.prompt: Put the rule checks the Signatures Tester evaluates in their documented order.
questions.explanation: The Tester runs the rules in this order. The first failure tells you where to look; later rules aren't evaluated until earlier ones pass. Note: the Diagnostic Logs article uses slightly different rule labels for the same concepts, the Tester's own UI labels are the canonical ones to read against.

questions.prompt: Which of the following does the Signatures Tester NOT evaluate? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The 'Identify Messages to Send to Exclaimer Cloud' transport rule in Exchange Online
The Senders rule on the Exclaimer signature
Google Workspace Content Compliance rules
The Exclaimer Exchange Transport Agent
Whether the signature is enabled for server-side
questions.explanation: The Tester only evaluates Exclaimer's own rules (Senders, Exceptions, Recipients, Date/Time, Advanced, plus the enabled toggle). Upstream Microsoft and Google rules can still block mail from reaching Exclaimer; for that you escalate to Diagnostic Logs.

questions.prompt: A Disclaimer is the same object as a signature, just rendered with plain text instead of HTML.
questions.explanation: A Disclaimer is a separate object that's appended after any active signature and any active Campaign. It's not a signature, it doesn't take a signature slot, and on Gmail it deploys server-side only.
